annotations.label: Postulate 1 (drawing a straight line)

anchor.section: Euclid's method and style of presentation
anchor.snippet: To draw a straight line from any point to any point

annotations.note: Mathlib defines affine spans/lines through points (e.g. AffineSubspace) but does not formalize Euclid's postulate of line construction as a stated axiom.

annotations.label: Postulate 3 (describing a circle)

anchor.section: Euclid's method and style of presentation
anchor.snippet: To describe a circle with any center and distance

annotations.note: Mathlib has Sphere/Metric.sphere as the analytic notion of a circle, but Euclid's construction postulate itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Pons asinorum (Book I, Prop. 5)

anchor.snippet: the base angles of an isosceles triangle are equal

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib stating that the base angles of an isosceles triangle are equal.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem (Book I, Props. 47–48)

anchor.snippet: the earliest surviving proof of the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Pythagorean theorem in inner-product/Euclidean form as an iff with the right-angle condition.

annotations.label: Golden ratio / extreme and mean ratio (Book II, Prop. 11)

anchor.snippet: subdivides a given line segment into extreme and mean proportions

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Real.goldenRatio := (1 + √5)/2 together with its irrationality and algebraic identities.

annotations.label: Inscribed angle theorem (Book III, Prop. 20)

anchor.snippet: the inscribed angle theorem relating inscribed to central angles

annotations.note: Mathlib states the central-angle = 2·inscribed-angle relation for oriented angles on a circle.

annotations.label: Thales's theorem (Book III, Prop. 31)

anchor.snippet: an angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle

annotations.note: Mathlib's Thales' theorem states angle p₁ p₂ p₃ = π/2 iff p₂ lies on the sphere with diameter p₁p₃.

annotations.label: Intersecting chords theorem (Book III, Prop. 35)

anchor.snippet: proposition 35 is the intersecting chords theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the intersecting chords theorem via cospherical points and the power-of-a-point file.

annotations.label: Tangent–secant theorem (Book III, Prop. 36)

anchor.snippet: proposition 36 is the tangent–secant theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib's Sphere.Power file proves the tangent–secant theorem.

annotations.label: Ratio of triangles/parallelograms of equal height (Book VI, Prop. 1)

anchor.snippet: Triangles and parallelograms which are under the same height are to one another as their bases

annotations.note: Mathlib has no synthetic statement that triangles/parallelograms of equal height have areas proportional to their bases.

annotations.label: Polygon similar to one and equal in area to another (Book VI, Prop. 25)

anchor.snippet: constructs, from any two polygons, a third polygon similar to the first and with the same area as the second

annotations.note: This synthetic construction of a polygon similar to one and equal in area to another is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Definition of a number (Book VII, Def. 2)

anchor.section: Books VII to X: Number theory
anchor.snippet: a number is a multitude composed of units

annotations.note: Mathlib uses Lean core's inductive Nat (zero/succ) rather than formalizing Euclid's philosophical 'multitude of units' definition.

annotations.label: Euclid's theorem on infinitude of primes (Book IX, Prop. 20)

anchor.snippet: the infinitude of prime numbers

annotations.note: Nat.exists_infinite_primes proves that for every n there is a prime p ≥ n, the standard form of Euclid's theorem.

annotations.label: Sum of a finite geometric series (Book IX, Prop. 35)

anchor.snippet: the formula for the sum of a finite geometric series

annotations.note: geom_sum_eq gives ∑ xⁱ = (xⁿ − 1)/(x − 1) in a field, the standard finite geometric-series formula.

annotations.label: Construction of even perfect numbers (Book IX, Prop. 36)

anchor.snippet: a construction using this sum for even perfect numbers

annotations.note: The Euclid construction 2^(p−1)(2^p − 1) → perfect when 2^p − 1 is prime is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect number

anchor.snippet: a number is perfect if it equals the sum of its proper divisors

annotations.note: Nat.Perfect n is defined exactly as the proper-divisor sum equalling n with n positive.

annotations.label: 28 as a perfect number

anchor.snippet: as for instance 28 = 1 + 2 + 4 + 7 + 14

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib explicitly asserts Nat.Perfect 28.

annotations.label: Euclid–Euler theorem

anchor.snippet: This result is the Euclid–Euler theorem

annotations.note: Mathlib has Nat.Perfect and Mersenne primes but no theorem characterising even perfect numbers as 2^(p−1)·(2^p − 1).

annotations.label: Irrationality of square roots of non-square integers (Book X, Prop. 9)

anchor.snippet: proves the irrationality of the square roots of all non-square integers

annotations.note: Irrational (√z) ↔ ¬IsSquare z for nonneg integers, which is precisely Book X Prop 9.

annotations.label: Euclid's formula for Pythagorean triples (Book X, lemma to Prop. 29)

anchor.snippet: Euclid's formula for producing all fundamental Pythagorean triples

annotations.note: Mathlib classifies primitive Pythagorean triples in the (m²−n², 2mn, m²+n²) form attributed to Euclid.

annotations.label: Euclid's notion of a rational line segment (Book X)

anchor.snippet: a line segment is rational when the square on its side has a rational area

annotations.note: Euclid's specific notion of a 'rational' segment (square-area rational) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Volume of cone is one-third volume of cylinder (Book XII)

anchor.snippet: the volume of a cone is a third of the volume of the corresponding cylinder

annotations.note: No declaration of cone volume = (1/3)·cylinder volume appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Volume of sphere proportional to cube of radius (Book XII)

anchor.snippet: the volume of a sphere is proportional to the cube of its radius

annotations.note: Mathlib computes the Lebesgue volume of the 3-dimensional Euclidean ball, exhibiting the r³ proportionality.

annotations.label: Classification of regular polyhedra (Book XIII)

anchor.snippet: these are the only regular polyhedra

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of regular polyhedra or the Platonic-solid classification.

annotations.label: Equal ratios of surfaces and volumes of dodecahedron and icosahedron (Book XIV)

anchor.snippet: the ratio of their surface areas and the ratio of their volumes are equal

annotations.note: No statement about dodecahedron/icosahedron surface and volume ratios is in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the ratio of their surface areas and the ratio of their volumes are equal

annotations.label: Parallel postulate (Book I, Postulate 5)

anchor.snippet: if a straight line falling on two straight lines make the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles

annotations.note: Mathlib develops Euclidean geometry over real inner-product spaces where the parallel property is implicit; the postulate is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Construction of equilateral triangle (Book I, Prop. 1) and implicit circle-intersection assumption

anchor.snippet: two circles sharing the same line segment as a radius will cross each other in two points

annotations.note: Neither the synthetic equilateral-triangle construction nor the two-circle intersection axiom is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean algorithm for GCD (Book VII, Props. 1–4)

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean algorithm, a method for determining whether numbers are relatively prime and for finding the greatest common divisor

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Nat.gcd by the Euclidean recursion gcd a b = gcd (b % a) a and proves the standard correctness lemmas (Nat.gcd_dvd_left, Nat.dvd_gcd, etc.).

annotations.label: Law of cosines (geometric precursor in Book II)

anchor.snippet: a geometric precursor of the law of cosines

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the law of cosines in inner-product space form, generalizing Euclid's geometric precursor in Book II (Propositions 12–13).

annotations.label: Constructible regular polygons (Book IV)

anchor.snippet: constructible regular polygons (i.e., those that have a straightedge and compass construction ) with 4, 5, 6, and 15 sides

annotations.note: Mathlib does not have a synthetic framework for straightedge-and-compass constructions or for constructibility of regular n-gons.

annotations.label: Impossibility of doubling the cube

anchor.snippet: doubling the cube , now known impossible with compass and straightedge

annotations.note: The impossibility of duplicating the cube by straightedge and compass is not formalized in Mathlib; the underlying constructible-number theory is also absent.

annotations.label: Method of exhaustion (Book XII)

anchor.snippet: the method of exhaustion , a precursor to integration

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Lebesgue/Bochner integration but does not give Euclid's classical method of exhaustion as a stated technique.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: any sound axiom system for set theory must necessarily be incomplete

annotations.note: Mathlib's model-theoretic framework underlies recent formalizations of Gödel's first incompleteness theorem; the statement in the article is an informal paraphrase.

annotations.label: Cross ratio (projective geometry)

anchor.snippet: the cross ratio (central to projective geometry )

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the projective cross ratio of four collinear points, the concept Zeeman observed is absent from Euclid's Book V.

annotations.label: Pasch's axiom

anchor.snippet: Pasch's axiom

annotations.note: Pasch's axiom — that a line entering a triangle through one side must exit through another — is not stated synthetically in Mathlib.
